gate_type: code execution required — WordPress add_filter() hook registration requires PHP code already loaded in the runtime (malicious plugin, compromised theme, or admin code injection); no unauthenticated HTTP vector exists to register filter hooks
reasoning: The two SSRF-capable code paths (fetch_and_cache_mappings and proxy_requests) are each guarded by Portal_Options::get_proxy_mappings_enabled(), a WordPress option that defaults to false and is only enabled when an admin connects to HubSpot. More critically, the exploitation mechanism itself — registering a malicious add_filter() callback for leadin_sites_proxy_cdn or leadin_plugin_mappings_api_url — requires PHP code execution within the WordPress runtime. WordPress filter hooks are PHP function calls that must be present in loaded plugin or theme files; they cannot be triggered by an unauthenticated HTTP request. An attacker therefore needs either: (1) an already-installed malicious plugin, (2) a compromised theme, or (3) admin-level write access (e.g. via theme/plugin editor, file upload). This is a plugin-pivot or supply-chain risk, not a standalone unauthenticated or subscriber-level web vulnerability. No in-plugin mechanism exposes or leaks the filter hook name in a way that aids exploitation — the hook names are static strings in PHP source. Severity is downgraded to LOW because exploitation is fully gated behind prior code-execution access at admin level, which itself represents a more severe compromise than the SSRF it enables.
